topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the locker room felt thick, suffocating. I kept my eyes fixed on the scuff marks on the polished floor, tracing patterns that offered no comfort. Coach Miller’s voice, low and steady, cut through the silence. "Marcus, this isn't working for the team anymore." My hands clenched into fists inside my tracksuit pockets, the fabric digging into my palms. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up, a cold, heavy weight settling in my chest. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush creeping up my jawline that I desperately tried to mask by staring intently at my shoes. When he finished speaking, the sound seemed amplified in the small space. I didn't look up. I just nodded once, a jerky, minimal movement, and started gathering my gear, the movement stiff and unnatural.

[story 2]

I walked down the hallway feeling like I was wading through deep water. The fluorescent lights hummed overhead, casting a harsh, unforgiving glare on my face. Each footfall echoed too loudly against the linoleum. I avoided the eyes of the other players, focusing instead on the worn texture of my own worn sneakers. When Coach called my name, the sound hit me like a physical blow. I stopped dead, my breath catching somewhere in my throat. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a strange, clammy sensation on my skin. When I finally met his gaze, my eyes dropped instantly to the floor, unable to hold the connection. "I understand, Coach," I managed, the words sounding thin and brittle, like old glass about to shatter.

[story 3]

The silence after the announcement was deafening. I stood by the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, each drop distorting the view of the empty field. My jaw was tight, muscles vibrating beneath the skin. I kept running a hand over my short hair, a repetitive, nervous gesture. The memory of the final practice, the missed pass, flashed behind my eyes, and a tightness constricted my throat until it felt like I couldn't draw a full breath. When the team started dispersing, I didn't move immediately. I stayed rooted, a statue of awkward stillness, feeling every glance from my teammates—a collective, heavy scrutiny that made my skin prickle with heat. I finally turned, the motion slow and reluctant, and walked toward the exit, the sound of my own footsteps feeling monumental.
